interactions.label: Show Input Channels
interactions.teaches: Toggle visibility of input channels to understand how each contributes to the final feature map.
Display detailed information about the feature map values and their origins from the input and filter.
Manipulate the filter kernel position to see its dynamic effect on the output feature map.

demo_steps.title: Overview of Conv Layers
demo_steps.narration: Here we have an input represented by distinct channels, a filter represented by a kernel, and the output feature map produced by the convolution operation. Each color represents a separate channel of the input.

demo_steps.title: Channel Contributions
demo_steps.narration: Notice how each input channel influences the output feature map when the filter kernel scans over it. The visibility toggle allows you to isolate specific channels to examine their effect on the convolution result.

demo_steps.title: Dynamic Kernel Application
demo_steps.narration: Interactively drag the filter kernel across the input to visualize how the output feature map changes. This helps in understanding the localized effects of the convolution process.

plan.camera_suggestion: Top-down orthographic view directly above the planes ensuring label readability and alignment with the original figure layout, with minimal perspective distortion.

plan.contextChunk:
Putting both of the above together, we can define a general
convolutional layer that maps a signal with $C_{\texttt{in}}$ input
channels to a signal with $C_{\texttt{out}}$ output channels. Here is
what this looks like for an image
$\mathbf{x}_{\texttt{in}}\in \mathbb{R}^{C_{\texttt{in}}\times N \times M}$,
where $c_2$ indexes the output channel, with
$c_2 \in \{0, \ldots, C_{\texttt{out}}-1\}$: 

$$\begin{aligned}\mathbf{x}_{\texttt{out}}[c_{\texttt{2}},:,:] = \sum_{c_{\texttt{1}}=1}^{C_{\texttt{in}}} \mathbf{w}[c_{\texttt{1}},c_{\texttt{2}},:,:] \star \mathbf{x}_{\texttt{in}}[c_{\texttt{1}},:,:] + b[c_{\texttt{2}}] & \quad \triangleleft \quad\texttt{conv}\quad \text{(multi-in-out)}
\end{aligned}
$${#eq-convolutional_neural_nets-convolutional_layer_multichannel}

Notation for multichannel convolutions can get hard to
keep track of, so let's spell out a few of the pieces here, which are
also visualized in
@fig-convolutional_neural_networks-multichannel_conv:

-   $\mathbf{x}_{\texttt{in}}[c_{\texttt{1}},:,:]$ is the
    $c_{\texttt{1}}$-th channel of the input signal.

-   The filter bank is $C_{\texttt{out}}$ filters,
    $[\mathbf{w}[:,0,:,:], \ldots, \mathbf{w}[:,C_{\texttt{out}}-1,:,:]]$,
    each of which applies one convolutional filter per input channel and
    then sums the responses over all these filters.

-   This convolutional layer maps inputs
    $\mathbf{x}_{\texttt{in}}\in \mathbb{R}^{C_{\texttt{in}}\times N \times M}$
    to outputs
    $\mathbf{x}_{\texttt{out}}\in \mathbb{R}^{C_{\texttt{out}}\times N \times M}$.

-   The filter bank is represented by a tensor
    $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{C_{\texttt{in}}\times C_{\texttt{out}}\times K \times K}$,
    where $K$ is the (spatial, square) kernel size.

![Multichannel convolution.](figures/convolutional_neural_nets/multichannel_conv.png){width="65%" #fig-convolutional_neural_networks-multichannel_conv}

It's important to get comfortable with the shapes of the data and
parameter tensors that get processed through different neural
architectures. This is essential when designing and building these
architectures, and when analyzing and debugging them. Let's go through
an example with concrete numbers. Consider data
$\mathbf{x}_{\texttt{in}}$, which is an RGB image of size
$128 \times 128$ pixels. We will pass it through a convoluational layer
that applies a bank of $3 \times 3$ filters (this refers to the spatial
extent of the filters). We omit the bias terms for simplicity. The
output ends up being a $96 \times 128 \times 128$ tensor, as shown in
@fig-convolutional_neural_nets-multichannel_conv_diagram.

![A convolutional layer that applies a bank of $3 \times 3$ filters. How many parameters does each filter have? How many filters are in the filter bank? *Source*: created by Jonas Wulff.](figures/convolutional_neural_nets/multichannel_conv_diagram.png){width="65%" #fig-convolutional_neural_nets-multichannel_conv_diagram}

To check your understanding, you should be able to answer the following
questions:

1.  How many parameters does each filter have? (A) 9, (B) 27, (C)
    96, (D) 864

2.  How many filters are in the filter bank? (A) 3, (B) 27, (C) 96, (D)
    can't say

The answers are given in the footnote.[^1]

### Strided Convolution

Convolutional layers, as defined previously, maintain the spatial resolution of the signal they process. However, commonly it is sufficient, or even desirable, to output a lower resolution. This can be achieved with strided convolution:

elements: filter bank w with Cin * Cout filters
input signal xin with dimensions Cin x M
output signal xout with dimensions Cout x N
bracket annotations for Cin, Cout, K, M, and N
convolution operation symbol between filters and inputs
equal sign connecting input and output

interactions.label: C_in
interactions.teaches: Changing C_in adjusts the number of input channels represented in the input signal, demonstrating the effect of deeper input data.

interactions.label: C_out
interactions.teaches: Altering C_out changes the number of filters, showing how more filters produce more feature maps in the output.

demo_steps.title: Initial Setup
demo_steps.narration: Here's the convolutional mapping of a simple input with C_in=3 channels passing through a filter bank to produce an output with C_out=5 feature maps.

demo_steps.title: Increasing Input Channels
demo_steps.narration: By increasing C_in to 5, the input signal becomes deeper, meaning each filter has more data dimensions to analyze, which can lead to richer representations.

demo_steps.title: Expanding Output Filters
demo_steps.narration: Now, increase C_out to 10. More filters mean the network can capture a wider array of features, effectively increasing the model's expressiveness and capability.

demo_steps.title: Minimal Connections
demo_steps.narration: Set both C_in and C_out to 1. This scenario represents the simplest convolutional layer, basically a single filter applied to a single-channel input, like a basic grayscale image.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Front-facing orthographic view to show the parallel layers clearly, ensuring all bracketed annotations and dimensions are visible and readable.

interactions.label: C_in
interactions.teaches: Changing C_in alters the depth of the input stack, showing more layers being processed by the filters.

interactions.label: C_out
interactions.teaches: Adjusting C_out changes the number of output channels, illustrating how more filters produce a richer feature map.

demo_steps.title: Visualize Initial Mapping
demo_steps.narration: With C_in=3 and C_out=3, see how each input channel interacts with filters to produce a layered output.

demo_steps.title: Depth in Input
demo_steps.narration: Increase C_in to 4, adding more layers to the input stack. This means each filter processes a deeper, more detailed input, potentially capturing complex patterns.

demo_steps.title: More Output Features
demo_steps.narration: Raise C_out to 5. Notice the output stack has additional layers, representing more detected features from the same input.

demo_steps.title: Single Channel Simplification
demo_steps.narration: Reduce both C_in and C_out to 1. This simplifies our system to the basics: a single-channel image processed into a single feature map, useful for basic scenarios like preprocessing grayscale images.

plan.camera_suggestion: Front-facing orthographic view with a slight 3D tilt to highlight the depth of the stacks, ensuring all annotations for Cin, Cout, and filters remain clear.
